example: Eva told a joke to break the ice at the party.
I need to hit the books before tomorrow's test.
Roxana spilled the beans about the surprise birthday party.
I'm a bit under the weather today, so I'll stay home.
That vocabulary quiz was a piece of cake!
We only eat out once in a blue moon.

idiom: Cost an arm and a leg
example: That laptop cost an arm and a leg.

idiom: The ball is in your court
example: I've explained the options, so the ball is in your court.
The engine repair failed, so we're back to square one.
Theresa burned the midnight oil finishing her thesis.
Nathan decided to bite the bullet and tell the boss he was quitting
Nico's sarcastic comment added fuel to the fire.

meaning: Something good that seemed bad at first
example: Missing the bus was a blessing in disguise because I met an old friend.
Stop beating around the bush and answer the question!

meaning: Doing something late is better than not doing it
example: Nacho finally submitted the assignment—better late than never.
We've done enough revision, let's call it a day.
The builders cut corners on the renovations, and now the roof is leaking.
Easy does it when you're carrying the wine bottles.
What began as a polite discussion quickly got out of hand, and everyone was shouting.
Hang in there—final exams are almost over.
Mafe was in hot water after missing the deadline.
Sebastian jumped the gun and announced the results early.
Can you keep an eye on my bag?

idiom: Let the cat out of the bag
example: Cristi let the cat out of the bag about Sam's wedding.
If you don't apply for that job soon, you'll miss the boat.
Angela kept practicing skateboarding—no pain, no gain.
Let's make sure the whole class is on the same page.
Relax! I'm just pulling your leg.
Speak of the devil—there's the professor now.

idiom: Take it with a grain of salt
example: Take that rumor with a grain of salt.
Our vacation plans are still up in the air.
It took time to wrap my head around calculus. Math is hard!

idiom: Your guess is as good as mine
example: Your guess is as good as mine about the exam date.
The detectives zeroed in on the main suspect after reviewing the evidence.

idiom: Hit the nail on the head
meaning: Describe exactly what is causing a situation
example: You hit the nail on the head when you said I need a vacation. I'm exhausted!
Rafael is a great guy, he is always ready to go the extra mile for his friends.
Once I learn the ropes at my new job, I won't be so nervous.
Mikhail is on thin ice after missing another class.
Ernesto called me out of the blue last night.

idiom: Put all your eggs in one basket
example: Don't put all your eggs in one basket—invest in a few different companies.
Thanks for the invite, but can I take a rain check? I'm swamped with work.
The doctor will be with you soon, just sit tight.
After months of saving, Natalia finally took the plunge and bought her first house.
After losing the election, Derek threw in the towel on his political career.
After the positive review, sales went through the roof.
Time flies when you're having fun!
I’ll touch base with the team before the meeting.
I spent an hour looking for my keys, and they were right under my nose! I had them in my pocket!

meaning: A past issue no longer important
example: We had a troubled past but that's all water under the bridge now.
Small businesses often rely on word of mouth instead of expensive advertising.
Failing the interview was a tough pill to swallow.

idiom: At the drop of a hat
example: I'd quit my job and move to the beach at the drop of a hat if I could.
She bent over backwards to make sure the guests felt welcome.
Bob is a great accountant. He does everything by the book.

idiom: Cross that bridge when you come to it
example: We might not have enough money for the trip, but let's cross that bridge when we come to it.
He gave me some really down to earth advice on how to manage my debt.

meaning: An obvious problem no one mentions
example: We all sat in the living room trying to ignore the elephant in the room: that mom had left dad for another man.
If you commit a crime, eventually you will have to face the music.
Adam is finally getting the hang of skateboarding.
Kevin tried to talk to Ashley at the party, but she gave him the cold shoulder all night.
The politican's career went down in flames after he was caught stealing charity money.
Buying a new car will save us money in the long run.
I know you didn't get the job, but keep your chin up—something better will come along.
No need for instructions, you all know the drill by now.
The detective left no stone unturned in the investigation.
Erika had to take a second job just to make ends meet.
He thought he would have to pay the fine, but the officer let him off the hook
Helen has been on cloud nine ever since she got that promotion at work.
Let's play it by ear depending on the weather. We may have to cancel the picnic.
She finally pulled the plug on her failing business.
Democrats and Republicans will never see eye to eye.
Heath Ledger as Joker stole the show in the Batman movie.
Thanks for the invite, but I'll have to take a rain check on dinner tonight—I have too much work.
If you want to get noticed, you have to think outside the box.
Teachers were turning a blind eye to smoking in school.
The company is under pressure to improve its environmental policy.

meaning: Act very carefully to avoid upsetting someone
example: We have had to walk on eggshells ever since our boss got divorced. He's so quick to anger!
Cafes in Concepción are a dime a dozen.
Alexander McQueen's fashion shows were always ahead of the curve.
Tell me about your Europe trip! I'm all ears.
Sitting on this beach with a cold beer is truly as good as it gets.
I skipped a few workouts last week, but I'm going to get back on track this week.
Amanda prefers working behind the scenes rather than being in the spotlight.
Jeremy goes to the gym to blow off steam.
I sold the car for exactly what I paid for it, so I broke even on the deal.
The government corruption was brought to light by a New York Times journalist.
If you want to join our trip to Torres del Paine, by all means, come along!
I asked my supervisor to meet so I could clear the air about my recent absences.
That umbrella will come in handy if it starts to rain later.
I'll try almost any food, but I draw the line at eating insects.
I was going to try bungee jumping but I got cold feet.
I have never tried skiing, but I’m going to give it a shot this weekend!

meaning: Adapt to events as they happen
example: I don't have any specific plans for the weekend; I'm just going to go with the flow.

meaning: Reach a point where progress stops
example: After running 20 miles in the marathon, Jimmy hit a wall and had to stop.
None of us have any money, so we're all in the same boat.
We don't have a job available right now, but we'll keep you in mind if a position opens up.
Long story short, we missed the flight because the car got a flat tire.
We're giving away free T-shirts! No strings attached!
I'm on the fence about which car to buy.
His behavior at the meeting was totally out of line, and he was asked to leave.
You have to read between the lines when reading poetry.
Does the name Patricia ring a bell?
It's a good rule of thumb to study a little every day so you don't stress later.
School was closed due to a snow day so I had an extra day to study for my exam. Saved by the bell!
After getting through the traffic, it was smooth sailing all the way home.

idiom: Take with a pinch of salt
example: When reading rumors about celebrities, it's best to take them with a pinch of salt.
She's always been rude to me, but it was the last straw when she started insulting my mother.
The hikers threw caution to the wind by climbing the mountain during the storm.
I need to get you up to speed on the new project.
She wears many hats: she's a doctor, a musician, and a writer.
'I hate politicians. 'You can say that again!'

meaning: People's intentions can be judged better by what they do than what they say.
example: John keeps promising to help, but actions speak louder than words, so I'll believe it when I see it.

meaning: To further a loss with mockery or indignity; to worsen an unfavourable situation.
example: My car broke down in the middle of nowhere, and then, to add insult to injury, it started to rain

meaning: Being rushed and having little time to complete something.
example: Emergency services were working against the clock as the flood waters began to rise.

meaning: Something that happens early or before it is supposed to.
example: We finished the project two days ahead of time.

meaning: At any time of the day or night
example: The patient required around the clock care after the surgery.
I can't believe it's already December; Christmas is just around the corner!.

meaning: An approach that uses traditional ideas that have previously worked.
example: The restaurant is getting back to basics by using fresh, simple, and few ingredients.

meaning: When an attempt fails and it's time to start all over.
example: The client hated the proposal, so it’s back to the drawing board for us.

meaning: Troublemaker or undesirable person in a group.
example: The gym is a big place with lots of people, so don't let one bad apple ruin your experience.

meaning: Looking in the wrong place. Accusing the wrong person.
example: If you’re asking me for legal advice, you’re barking up the wrong tree, bro. I’m a musician.

meaning: Do something quickly before it's too late.
example: We managed to beat the clock and arrived at the airport just minutes before boarding closed.

meaning: Carrying and idea that's constantly in your thoughts.
example: Mom has a bee in her bonnet about remodeling the kitchen before summer.
Elaine so behind the times; she refuses to use a smartphone.
The new Rolls-Royce comes with all the bells and whistles... including massage seats and a built-in espresso maker.

meaning: Enjoying the advantage of two things simultaneously.
example: By living in the suburbs but working in the city, Margarita gets the best of both worlds.
This new app is the best thing since sliced bread!

meaning: Waiting for further developments before taking action or making decisions.
example: Emily decided to bide her time until a better job offer came along.
Make sure your desk is tidy and your report is finished; I heard the big cheese is visiting next week.

idiom: Bird in the hand is worth two in the bush
meaning: Better to be satisfied with what you have than risk losing it by trying to get something better.
example: Don't sell your car before you have the money for a new one; a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

idiom: Bite off more than you can chew
meaning: To take on a task that is way too big.
example: I think Nico has bitten off more than he can chew taking all those classes this semester.
Mark was black and blue after being jumped by street thugs.
John has always been the black sheep of the family; while everyone else became doctors, he chose to be a struggling artist.
Many people in the industry were blacklisted for their political opinions.

meaning: Extort or take money from someone by threatening to use their secrets against them
example: Ariana blackmailed Steve for years by threatening to tell the newspapers about their affair.

meaning: Pre-arranged social interaction between people who have never met
example: Karen and Spencer met on a blind date and married in 2011.

meaning: Something that requires a lot of effort and hard work
example: The athlete gave their blood, sweat, and tears in training to qualify for the marathon.

meaning: Suddenly get very angry, perhaps over something unexpected
example: Ariadna blew a fuse when she found out her car had been stolen.

meaning: A plan or project that suddenly fails.
example: Chile's plan to raise public transportation fees blew up in their face when nationwide protests broke out.

meaning: From noble, aristocratic or wealthy family.
example: He acts like a blue blood, demanding the best service in every restaurant.

meaning: Working in a manual labor job
example: They are trying to win over blue-collar voters in the upcoming election.

meaning: Try hard to win an agreement but usually unsuccessful
example: Kathy argued with the store manager until she was blue in the face, but Falabella refused to give her a refund.

meaning: Superior quality or distinction. The best of a group
example: Our pumpkin won the blue ribbon at the county fair this year
Sarah is a total bookworm; she can finish two novels in a single weekend.

idiom: Born with a silver spoon in one's mouth
meaning: Born into a rich and affluent family
example: Jared has never worked hard for anything because he was born with a silver spoon in his mouth.

meaning: A plan or project that fails, or loses all value
example: When the housing market crashed in 2008, the bottom fell out of home prices.

meaning: Develop or think of new ideas
example: We need to brainstorm some new marketing strategies before the meeting.

meaning: Person that is the primary source of income for a family.
example: As a single mom, Kathia was the sole breadwinner in her household.
I would love to take a vacation, but I have to work to bring home the bacon.

idiom: Broken watch is right twice a day
meaning: When something is lucky or successful but undeserving.
example: Leopold is a moron but even a broken watch is right twice a day.

meaning: Working hard with determination and full attention
example: Elena decided it was time to buckle down as finals week approached.
After years of trying, they were thrilled to finally have a bun in the oven

meaning: Flatter someone, usually because you want something from them
example: She tried to butter up her boss by complimenting his suit, hoping he would approve her vacation request.

meaning: Something happens of develops gradually or little by little
example: The crowd at the music festival was thinning, but only by degrees.

meaning: Having strong feelings for someone who cannot be yours
example: Frank may be engaged to Helen, but I think he still carries a torch for Laura.
The iPhone is a cash cow for the Apple corporation.
Maria Teresa was so quiet, moving through the house like a cat burglar.

meaning: Catch someone in the act of committing a crime or doing something they shouldn't
example: The kids were caught red-handed eating cookies before dinner.

meaning: Try to achieve something that is very difficult, impossible or very desirable
example: Eugene has been chasing rainbows, thinking he'll become a millionaire overnight.

meaning: Spend a lot of time and energy but achieve nothing
example: Stop changing the PowerPoint colors! You're just chasing your tail and going to miss the deadline.
That's the cheesiest pick-up line I've ever heard.

meaning: A comment that is true and makes you uncomfortable
example: The message the film conveyed struck so hard and so close to home that it moved me to tears.

meaning: When someonne develops and reaches maturity
example: Superbad is one of the funniest coming of age movies.

meaning: Spoil the person's chance of success
example: Universidad de Concepción was already trailing, and that last goal really cooked their goose.

meaning: Someone who copies the work of another
example: She bought the same dress as me; she's such a copycat.

meaning: Lazy person who watches too much TV
example: My dad is such a couch potato. He's even trained the dog to brings him beers from the fridge!

meaning: Complete a lot of work or a wide range of things
example: Guys, that was a great study session. We covered a lot of ground.
Jerrod wakes up at the crack of dawn for football practice.

meaning: A point at which something difficult must be done
example: Steven plays well without pressure, but can he produce at crunch time?

meaning: Complain about a loss from the past
example: There's no point crying over spilt milk. Sure, Alonso cheated on you but it's time to get over it.

meaning: Being inquisitive can lead you into an unpleasant or dangerous situation
example: Let's not try to find out what's in the abandoned house. Curiosity killed the cat.

meaning: Doing something that will cause your own failure or downfall
example: Corrupt politicians are cutting their own throat.
I couldn't concentrate on the exam because the guy in front of me kept passing gas!
I don't have time for this, so let's cut to the chase: are we losing money or not?
See? These are the type of crazy customers I deal with on a day to day basis!

meaning: Expected to be in a role or to die soon
example: If you keep driving like a lunatic, your days are numbered.

meaning: Plan or project that has ceased to function and is not expected to re-activate.
example: Without additional funding, the project is dead in the water and cannot proceed.

meaning: People or things that are no longer useful or necessary
example: The company is full of dead wood. They need to reinvent themselves.

meaning: Describing what a person really feels deep inside them
example: She can seem stern but deep down she's a very kind person.

meaning: Do what is expected or required
example: Tony Soprano always delivers the goods. Not to worry.
I'll play devil's advocate and point out the flaws in your plan.
It's not always easy to find a job after you've done time.

idiom: Don't count your chickens before they hatch
meaning: Don't make plans for something that might not happen.
example: I know the interview went well, but don't count your chickens before they hatch; wait until you get the offer letter.

idiom: Don't give up your day job
meaning: You are not very good at something
example: Wow, your singing is terrible. Don't give up your day job.

meaning: Be unable to come up with an answer or dia
example: Rodrigo asked me for my phone number and I drew a blank—I couldn't remember it.

meaning: Stop attending school before it is time.
example: He chose to drop out of college to pursue his passion for music.

meaning: Thinking too much about something that happened in the past.
example: I wish my grandfather did not dwell so much on the past... but he is a Vietnam War veteran.
Mark is such an eager beaver; he's always the first one in the office and the last to leave for the day.

meaning: Being so enthusiastic and passionate about something that you think about it all the time.
example: Marco eat, sleeps, and breathes football. He hopes to get a sports scholarship to Stanford.
Karina was dismissed as an egghead by her colleagues just because she preferred studying over socializing.

meaning: Enough space to move or work in.
example: It's a small kitchen with no elbow room.

meaning: At the very last minute or just in time.
example: Tammy submitted her final project at the eleventh hour, just minutes before the midnight deadline.

idiom: Every cloud has a silver lining
meaning: Be optimistic, even difficult times will lead to better days. Good can come from a bad situation.
example: I know you're upset about losing your phone, but every cloud has a silver lining—now you can finally upgrade to a newer model.
The police will explore every avenue to find the missing child.

meaning: a person who is physically attractive, or an object that is pleasant and visually appealing
example: I don't know his name, but the new actor in that movie is very easy on the eyes.
